seasonalPresence: Year-round. Rut in October. Best viewing autumn.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk. Martindale and the eastern fells.
whereToSee: Martindale is the place for red deer. The valley is a stronghold. Also Grizedale Forest, the Ullswater area, and the eastern fells. Rut in October is spectacular.
ukPopulation: Around 360,000 in the UK. Largest land mammal in Britain.
lakesPopulation: Martindale holds one of the most accessible populations in England. Grizedale and Ullswater too. You'll hear the rut before you see it.
description: Red deer are the monarchs of the Lakes. Martindale is the place. Dawn or dusk in October for the rut. You'll hear the stags roaring. Keep your distance. They're wild. The valley is a designated refuge.
identification: Large. Red-brown in summer, grey in winter. Stags have antlers. Hinds smaller. Unmistakeable.
tips: Martindale. October for the rut. Dawn or dusk. Take binoculars. Keep your distance. Join a guided walk if you want to get closer.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most visible at dawn and dusk.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk. Or after rain when they're more active.
whereToSee: Rivers and lakes throughout the Lakes. The Derwent, Rothay, and the shores of Windermere and Ullswater. Look for tracks and spraints.
ukPopulation: Around 10,000 in England. Recovered from near extinction.
lakesPopulation: Present on most Lakeland rivers and lakes. Elusive. Dawn and dusk. Look for tracks on muddy banks.
description: Otters are back in the Lakes. They're elusive. Dawn and dusk. The Derwent, Rothay, Windermere shores. Look for tracks and spraints. You might get lucky. Patience pays.
identification: Long, sinuous. Brown fur. Flat tail. Webbed feet. Tracks show five toes.
tips: Dawn and dusk. Quiet stretches of river. Look for tracks on muddy banks. Spraints smell of fish and jasmine. Don't expect to see one. Be pleased if you do.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most active in morning and late afternoon.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. First few hours after dawn.
whereToSee: Grizedale Forest is the stronghold. Also Whinlatter, the Borrowdale valley, and conifer woodland throughout the Lakes. Feeders at visitor centres help.
ukPopulation: Around 140,000 in the UK. Declining. Grey squirrel competition and disease.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale holds one of England's best populations. Whinlatter too. The Lake District is a stronghold. Feeders at the visitor centres.
description: Red squirrels are the Lakes. Grizedale is the place. The visitor centre has feeders. Whinlatter too. Go in the morning. They're used to people. That ear tufts. No grey squirrels here. They've been kept out.
identification: Red-brown fur. Ear tufts. Bushy tail. No grey. Smaller than grey squirrel.
tips: Grizedale visitor centre. Morning. The feeders. Whinlatter has a red squirrel trail. Conifer woodland. They're easier to see than you think.

bestTimeOfDay: Dusk. Or use a trail camera.
whereToSee: Grizedale Forest, Whinlatter, the Borrowdale valley. Reintroduced to the Lakes. Elusive. Trail cameras at some sites.
ukPopulation: Around 3,700 in Britain. Mostly Scotland. Reintroduced to England.
lakesPopulation: Reintroduced to Grizedale and Whinlatter. Present but elusive. Trail cameras at the visitor centres sometimes catch them.
description: Pine martens are back in the Lakes. Reintroduced. Grizedale, Whinlatter. You won't see one. They're nocturnal. Trail cameras at the visitor centres. Check the screens. That's your best chance.
identification: Cat-sized. Brown fur. Cream throat patch. Long bushy tail. Weasel family.
tips: Trail cameras at Grizedale and Whinlatter visitor centres. Dusk if you're lucky. Don't expect to see one. They're elusive.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most active at dusk and dawn.
whereToSee: Woodland edge throughout the Lakes. Setts in banks. Don't disturb. Watch from a distance.
lakesPopulation: Common in Lakeland woodland. Setts in banks. Dusk is the time. Keep your distance. Don't use lights.
description: Badgers are everywhere in the Lakes. Woodland edge. Setts in banks. Dusk. They're shy. Don't disturb the sett. Watch from a distance. No lights. Let your eyes adjust.
identification: Grey, black and white striped face. Stocky. Low to the ground. Unmistakeable.
tips: Dusk. Woodland edge. Find a sett first in daylight. Don't approach. Watch from distance. No lights.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. White in winter, brown in summer.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk. Or in winter when snow makes them visible.
whereToSee: High fells. The Helvellyn range, the Coniston fells, the eastern fells. Above the treeline. Introduced to the Lakes.
ukPopulation: Around 350,000 in Scotland. Small introduced populations in England.
lakesPopulation: Introduced to the Lake District fells. Helvellyn, Coniston Old Man. White in winter. Brown in summer. High ground.
description: Mountain hares live on the high fells. Helvellyn, Coniston. White in winter. Brown in summer. They're shy. You need to walk. Dawn and dusk. Or winter when the snow makes them stand out.
identification: Smaller than brown hare. White in winter. Brown in summer. Shorter ears than brown hare.
tips: High fells. Winter for white coat. Dawn and dusk. They're shy. Walk quietly.
